title: Week 8: Łukasiewicz Logic and McNaughton's Theorem

questions.text: Which of the following correctly states the truth function of conjunction ($\wedge$) in Łukasiewicz logic $\mathbb{F}^3$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F_{\wedge}(x,y) = \max(0, x+y-1)$
$F_{\wedge}(x,y) = \min(x,y)$
$F_{\wedge}(x,y) = xy$
$F_{\wedge}(x,y) = \min(1, x+y)$

explanations.text: In $\mathbb{F}^3$, conjunction is defined as $\max(0, x+y-1)$.
$\min(x,y)$ is the conjunction of Gödel logic $\mathbb{F}^1$, not $\mathbb{F}^3$.
$xy$ (the product) is the conjunction of Product logic $\mathbb{F}^2$, not $\mathbb{F}^3$.
$\min(1, x+y)$ is the disjunction of $\mathbb{F}^3$, not the conjunction.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly states the truth function of implication ($\to$) in Łukasiewicz logic $\mathbb{F}^3$? (Select ALL that apply.)

explanations.text: $\max(1-x, y)$ is the implication of Gödel logic $\mathbb{F}^1$.
$1-x+xy$ is the implication of Product logic $\mathbb{F}^2$.
In $\mathbb{F}^3$, implication is $\min(1, 1-x+y)$.
$\max(0, y-x)$ is not a standard fuzzy implication in any of $\mathbb{F}^1$, $\mathbb{F}^2$, $\mathbb{F}^3$.

questions.source_ref: Section 1.2: The Law of Excluded Middle (LEM)
questions.text: The Law of Excluded Middle (LEM) states that $x \vee \neg x = 1$ for all $x \in [0,1]$. Which of the following fuzzy logics satisfy LEM? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Łukasiewicz logic $\mathbb{F}^3$
Gödel logic $\mathbb{F}^1$
Product logic $\mathbb{F}^2$
Classical propositional logic

explanations.text: Under $\mathbb{F}^3$: $x \vee \neg x = \min(1, x+(1-x)) = \min(1,1) = 1$.
Under $\mathbb{F}^1$: $x \vee \neg x = \max(x, 1-x)$, which equals $\frac{1}{2}$ at $x = \frac{1}{2}$, not 1.
Under $\mathbb{F}^2$: $x \vee \neg x = 1 - x(1-x)$, which equals $\frac{3}{4}$ at $x = \frac{1}{2}$, not 1.
Classical logic is two-valued, and $p \vee \neg p$ is a tautology: both $p=0$ and $p=1$ give $\max(p,1-p)=1$ in the two-value setting.

questions.source_ref: Section 1.3: Łukasiewicz Logic Extends Gödel Logic
questions.text: In $\mathbb{F}^3$, defined operators $\underline{\wedge}$ and $\underline{\vee}$ are introduced. Which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $x \underline{\wedge} y = (x \to y) \wedge x$ and its truth function is $\min(x,y)$
$x \underline{\vee} y = (x \to y) \to y$ and its truth function is $\max(x,y)$
$\underline{\wedge}$ and $\underline{\vee}$ allow $\mathbb{F}^3$ to replicate all reasoning done in $\mathbb{F}^1$
$\underline{\wedge}$ and $\underline{\vee}$ also allow $\mathbb{F}^3$ to replicate all reasoning done in $\mathbb{F}^2$

explanations.text: By definition and verified computation, $x \underline{\wedge} y = (x \to y) \wedge x = \min(x,y)$.
By definition, $x \underline{\vee} y = (x \to y) \to y = \max(x,y)$.

Formally, there is a translation $t: \mathrm{Fml} \to \mathrm{Fml}$ such that $\models^{\mathbb{F}^1} F$ iff $\models^{\mathbb{F}^3} t(F)$, replacing $\wedge$ with $\underline{\wedge}$ and $\vee$ with $\underline{\vee}$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathbb{F}^3$ is an extension of $\mathbb{F}^1$: any reasoning done in $\mathbb{F}^1$ can be replicated in $\mathbb{F}^3$

explanations.text: No such translation exists for $\mathbb{F}^2$ inside $\mathbb{F}^3$: the $\mathbb{F}^2$ t-norm cannot be expressed by any finite combination of $\mathbb{F}^3$ connectives.
explanations.notes_quote: no such translation exists for $\mathbb{F}^2$ inside $\mathbb{F}^3$

questions.text: In classical propositional logic, every Boolean function $f: \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$ is representable as a formula. Which of the following statements about the analogous question in Łukasiewicz logic $\mathbb{F}^3$ are true? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Not every function $f: [0,1]^n \to [0,1]$ is representable in $\mathbb{F}^3$
There are $2^{2^{\aleph_0}}$ functions $f: [0,1] \to [0,1]$ but only $\aleph_0$ formulas, so most functions are not representable
Every function $f: [0,1]^n \to [0,1]$ is representable by some formula under $\mathbb{F}^3$
In classical logic, any Boolean function can be constructed via DNF (Disjunctive Normal Form)

explanations.text: McNaughton's theorem restricts representable functions to those that are piecewise-linear with integer coefficients — a tiny fraction of all real-valued functions.
explanations.notes_quote: For arbitrary real-valued functions $f : [0,1]^n \to [0,1]$, the same is not true under Łukasiewicz semantics

explanations.text: A cardinality argument: there are $2^{2^{\aleph_0}}$ such functions but only countably many formulas, so only countably many functions are representable.
explanations.notes_quote: there are $2^{2^{\aleph_0}}$ such functions but only $\aleph_0$ formulas

explanations.text: This is false: only continuous piecewise-linear functions with integer coefficients are representable.
Every Boolean function can be written as a DNF formula, using $p_i$ for $x_i=1$ and $\neg p_i$ for $x_i=0$.
explanations.notes_quote: every Boolean function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$ is the truth function of some formula (constructible via DNF)

questions.text: The clamping notation $[z]^1_0$ is defined as $\min(1, \max(0, z))$. Which of the following correctly describe its behaviour? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $[z]^1_0 = z$ if $0 \leq z \leq 1$
$[z]^1_0 = z$ for all $z \in \mathbb{R}$

explanations.text: When $0 \leq z \leq 1$, $\max(0,z)=z$ and $\min(1,z)=z$.
When $z \geq 1$, $\max(0,z)=z$ and $\min(1,z)=1$.
For $z$ outside $[0,1]$ (e.g. $z=2$), $[z]^1_0 = 1 \neq z$. The function clamps rather than being identity everywhere.

questions.text: McNaughton's Theorem (1 Variable) characterises exactly which functions $f: [0,1] \to [0,1]$ are truth functions of propositional formulas under $\mathbb{F}^3$. Which of the following conditions must hold for a function $f$ to be representable? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $f$ must be continuous on $[0,1]$
$f$ must be piecewise linear (finitely many linear pieces)
Each linear piece must have integer slope and intercept
$f$ must be monotone (non-decreasing or non-increasing)
Adjacent linear pieces must join continuously (no jumps at breakpoints)

explanations.text: Continuity is required by McNaughton's theorem.
explanations.notes_quote: a function is representable in $\mathbb{F}^3$ if and only if it is a continuous piecewise-linear function with integer coefficients

explanations.text: There must be a finite partition $0=\gamma_0 < \gamma_1 < \cdots < \gamma_n = 1$ with $f$ linear on each piece.
explanations.notes_quote: there exists an increasing sequence $0 = \gamma_0 < \gamma_1 < \gamma_2 < \cdots < \gamma_n = 1$ and a family of linear functions

explanations.text: Each piece has $f_i(x) = a_i x + b_i$ with $a_i, b_i \in \mathbb{Z}$.
Monotonicity is not required. For example, the V-shaped function $(p \wedge p) \vee \neg p$ is representable but not monotone.
Continuity at breakpoints is explicitly required: $f_i(\gamma_{i+1}) = f_{i+1}(\gamma_{i+1})$ for all $i$.
explanations.notes_quote: the pieces join continuously: $f_i(\gamma_{i+1}) = f_{i+1}(\gamma_{i+1})$ for all $i < n$

sections.title: McNaughton's Theorem Part 2 — Proof of Lemma 8.2

questions.text: Lemma 8.2 establishes one direction of McNaughton's Theorem. Which direction does it prove, and what does it state? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It proves the 'only if' direction: every representable function has integer-coefficient piecewise-linear truth function
It proves the 'if' direction: every CPLIC function is representable
The proof proceeds by structural induction on formula size
The proof proceeds by induction on the number of breakpoints

explanations.text: Lemma 8.2 proves that every truth function of a formula is a CPLIC function — the 'only if' direction.
explanations.notes_quote: This establishes the 'only if' direction of McNaughton's Theorem

explanations.text: The 'if' direction (every CPLIC function is representable) is established by Lemma 8.3 and the Tree Algorithm.
The proof proceeds by induction on the size (number of connectives) of the formula.
explanations.notes_quote: Proof by Induction on Formula Size

explanations.text: Induction on breakpoints is not the method used; structural induction on formula construction is.

questions.source_ref: Section 3.2: Proof by Induction on Formula Size
questions.text: In the proof of Lemma 8.2 by structural induction, consider the case $F = G \wedge H$ where both $G$ and $H$ have CPLIC truth functions with a common partition. What is the resulting linear piece for $F$ on each sub-interval? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $f_i(x) = (a_i + c_i)x + (b_i + d_i - 1)$, which has integer coefficients
$f_i(x) = \min(g_i(x), h_i(x))$, taking the pointwise minimum
The coefficients of $f_i$ are integers because sums and differences of integers are integers
$f_i(x) = (a_i \cdot c_i)x + (b_i \cdot d_i)$, taking the product

explanations.text: Under $\mathbb{F}^3$, $G \wedge H = \max(0, g(x)+h(x)-1)$. On each sub-interval this gives $f_i(x) = (a_i+c_i)x + (b_i+d_i-1)$.
explanations.notes_quote: $f_i(x) = (a_i + c_i)x + (b_i + d_i - 1)$ has integer coefficients $a_i + c_i \in \mathbb{Z}$ and $b_i + d_i - 1 \in \mathbb{Z}$

explanations.text: $\min(g,h)$ is the Gödel conjunction ($\mathbb{F}^1$), not the Łukasiewicz conjunction ($\mathbb{F}^3$).
Since $a_i, c_i, b_i, d_i \in \mathbb{Z}$, we have $a_i+c_i \in \mathbb{Z}$ and $b_i+d_i-1 \in \mathbb{Z}$.
explanations.notes_quote: has integer coefficients $a_i + c_i \in \mathbb{Z}$ and $b_i + d_i - 1 \in \mathbb{Z}$

explanations.text: Product of coefficients is not used; the Łukasiewicz conjunction adds the argument functions and subtracts 1.

questions.source_ref: Section 3.2: Proof by Induction — Negation Case
questions.text: In the inductive proof of Lemma 8.2, consider the case $F = \neg G$ where $G$ has linear piece $g_i(x) = a_i x + b_i$. Which of the following correctly describe the linear piece for $F$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The new coefficients $-a_i$ and $1-b_i$ are integers because $a_i, b_i \in \mathbb{Z}$
The partition $\{\gamma_i\}$ for $F$ may be different from that for $G$
$f_i(x) = 1 - g_i(x)$ everywhere on $[\gamma_i, \gamma_{i+1}]$

explanations.text: Negation in $\mathbb{F}^3$ is $1 - g_i(x) = 1 - (a_i x + b_i) = (-a_i)x + (1-b_i)$.
explanations.notes_quote: Define $f_i(x) = a'_i x + b'_i$ where $a'_i = -a_i \in \mathbb{Z}$ and $b'_i = (1 - b_i) \in \mathbb{Z}$

explanations.text: The negatives and shifted values of integers remain integers.
The same partition $\{\gamma_i\}$ is used for $F = \neg G$ as for $G$; only the linear functions change.
By definition of $\mathbb{F}^3$ negation, $\overline{F}(x) = 1 - \overline{G}(x) = 1 - g_i(x)$ on each piece.

sections.title: McNaughton's Theorem Part 3 — Lemma 8.3 and Tree Algorithm

questions.text: Lemma 8.3 states that for any integers $n, m \in \mathbb{Z}$, the function $f_{n,m}(x) = [nx+m]^1_0$ is the truth function of some formula $F_{n,m}$. What does this lemma establish about McNaughton's Theorem? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It shows that any single clamped linear function with integer coefficients is representable in $\mathbb{F}^3$
It establishes the converse direction for individual building blocks of piecewise-linear functions
Together with a combination argument, it proves the full 'if' direction of McNaughton's Theorem
It proves that every CPLIC function is the truth function of a formula with exactly $|n|$ connectives

explanations.text: Lemma 8.3 shows each single piece $[nx+m]^1_0$ is individually representable.
explanations.notes_quote: any single clamped linear function with integer coefficients is representable

explanations.text: The converse direction of McNaughton's theorem requires showing each building block is representable, which Lemma 8.3 does.
explanations.notes_quote: This establishes the converse direction for the building blocks

explanations.text: Lemma 8.3 plus the combination using $\underline{\wedge}$ and $\underline{\vee}$ yields the full converse.
There is no claim that $|n|$ connectives suffice; the tree algorithm may produce more complex formulas.

questions.source_ref: Section 4.2: Proof of Lemma 8.3 by Induction
questions.text: In the base case ($n = 0$) of the inductive proof of Lemma 8.3, what formulas are used? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F_{0,m} = \bot$ (contradiction, e.g. $p \wedge \neg p$) when $m \leq 0$
$F_{0,m} = \top$ (tautology, e.g. $p \vee \neg p$) when $m \geq 1$
$F_{0,m} = p$ (the propositional variable) for all values of $m$
The truth function $f_{0,m}(x) = [m]^1_0$ is a constant function (either 0 or 1)

explanations.text: When $n=0$ and $m \leq 0$: $[m]^1_0 = 0$, so we use the contradiction formula $\bot = p \wedge \neg p$.
When $n=0$ and $m \geq 1$: $[m]^1_0 = 1$, so we use the tautology formula $\top = p \vee \neg p$.
$p$ has truth function $x$, not $[m]^1_0$ which is constant. The base case uses $\bot$ or $\top$ depending on $m$.
When $n=0$, $f_{0,m}(x) = [m]^1_0$ is independent of $x$ and equals 0 (if $m \leq 0$) or 1 (if $m \geq 1$).

questions.source_ref: Section 4.2: Proof of Lemma 8.3 — Inductive Step
questions.text: In the inductive step for $n = k+1 > 0$ in the proof of Lemma 8.3, the formula $F_{(k+1),m}$ is defined using $F_{k,m}$ and $F_{k,(m+1)}$. Which of the following correctly describe this construction? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F_{(k+1),m} = (F_{k,m} \vee p) \wedge F_{k,(m+1)}$
$F_{(k+1),m} = (F_{k,m} \wedge p) \vee F_{k,(m+1)}$
For negative $n = -(k+1)$, the formula uses $\neg p$ instead of $p$
$F_{-(k+1),m} = (F_{-k,(m-1)} \vee \neg p) \wedge F_{-k,m}$

explanations.text: The inductive construction for positive slope uses $(F_{k,m} \vee p) \wedge F_{k,(m+1)}$.
The order of $\vee$ and $\wedge$ is reversed from what is correct; $(F_{k,m} \wedge p) \vee F_{k,(m+1)}$ does not give $[{(k+1)x+m}]^1_0$.
For decreasing functions (negative $n$), $\neg p$ replaces $p$ in the recursive construction.
explanations.notes_quote: Induction step for $n = -(k+1) < 0$: Use $f_{-k,m}$ and $f_{-k,(m-1)}$, and define $F_{-(k+1),m} = (F_{-k,(m-1)} \vee \neg p) \wedge F_{-k,m}$

explanations.text: The negative-slope inductive formula is $F_{-(k+1),m} = (F_{-k,(m-1)} \vee \neg p) \wedge F_{-k,m}$.

questions.text: Regarding the Tree Algorithm for constructing formula $F_{n,m}$ for $[nx+m]^1_0$, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: When $n > 0$, each node branches into children with functions $(n-1)x+m$ (left) and $(n-1)x+(m+1)$ (right)
The parent formula when $n > 0$ is $(F_\text{left} \vee p) \wedge F_\text{right}$
When $n = 0$ and $m \leq 0$, the leaf formula is $\top$
When $n = 0$ and $m \geq 1$, the leaf formula is $\top$
$(F \wedge \top) \equiv F$ and $(F \vee \bot) \equiv F$ are simplification rules used in the algorithm

explanations.text: For positive $n$, the left child has function $(n-1)x+m$ and right child $(n-1)x+(m+1)$.
explanations.notes_quote: If $n > 0$: Left child: $(n-1)x + m$, Right child: $(n-1)x + (m+1)$

explanations.text: The parent formula combines the children as $(F_\text{left} \vee p) \wedge F_\text{right}$ when $n > 0$.
explanations.notes_quote: And the parent formula is: $(F_\text{left} \vee p) \wedge F_\text{right}$

explanations.text: When $n=0$ and $m \leq 0$, the formula is $\bot$ (falsum, truth function constantly 0), not $\top$.
When $n=0$ and $m \geq 1$, the leaf formula is $\top$ (truth function constantly 1).
These simplification rules reduce formula complexity when propagating base cases upward.

questions.text: Applying the tree algorithm to $[3x-2]^1_0$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The resulting formula is $F = p \wedge p \wedge p$
Verification: $p \wedge p$ has truth function $\max(0, 2x-1) = [2x-1]^1_0$
The resulting formula is $F = p \vee p \vee p$

explanations.text: After applying the tree algorithm and simplification rules, $[3x-2]^1_0$ corresponds to $p \wedge p \wedge p$.
explanations.notes_quote: Result: $[3x-2]^1_0$ corresponds to the formula $F = (p \wedge p \wedge p)$

explanations.text: Under $\mathbb{F}^3$: $p \wedge p = \max(0, x+x-1) = \max(0, 2x-1) = [2x-1]^1_0$.
Combining: $(p \wedge p) \wedge p = \max(0, [2x-1]^1_0 + x - 1)$. For $x \geq 1/2$: $= \max(0, 3x-2) = [3x-2]^1_0$.
$p \vee p \vee p = [3x]^1_0$, which is an increasing function, not $[3x-2]^1_0$.

questions.text: Applying the tree algorithm to $[2-3x]^1_0 = [-3x+2]^1_0$ (where $n=-3 < 0$), which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Since $n < 0$, the algorithm uses $\neg p$ branches instead of $p$

The root formula is $((
eg p \wedge 
eg p) \vee 
eg p) \wedge (
eg p \vee 
eg p)$
$\neg p \wedge \neg p$ has truth function $[-2x+1]^1_0 = [1-2x]^1_0$
The formula for $[-3x+2]^1_0$ is the same as for $[3x-2]^1_0$ with $p$ replaced by $\neg p$

explanations.text: For negative slopes, the tree algorithm uses $\neg p$ to generate the decreasing behaviour.

Working through the tree gives root formula $((
eg p \wedge 
eg p) \vee 
eg p) \wedge (
eg p \vee 
eg p)$.
explanations.notes_quote:
Root formula: $(((
eg p \wedge 
eg p) \vee 
eg p) \wedge (
eg p \vee 
eg p))$

explanations.text:
$
eg p$ has truth function $1-x$, so $
eg p \wedge 
eg p = \max(0, (1-x)+(1-x)-1) = \max(0, 1-2x) = [1-2x]^1_0$.
The formulas have different structures; they share the pattern of iterated $\wedge$ but the tree for $[-3x+2]^1_0$ involves $\vee$ of pieces as well.

questions.source_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Functions — Min of Two Linear Pieces
questions.text: To represent a piecewise-linear function that is the minimum of two clamped linear pieces $f_{3,-1}(x) = [3x-1]^1_0$ and $f_{-5,4}(x) = [-5x+4]^1_0$, which approach is used? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Use the defined operator $\underline{\wedge}$ (which realises $\min$): $F = F_{3,-1} \underline{\wedge} F_{-5,4}$
Use the Łukasiewicz $\wedge$ (which realises $\max(0, x+y-1)$) directly
Each piece $F_{n,m}$ is first constructed using the tree algorithm
Use the defined operator $\underline{\vee}$ (which realises $\max$) to combine the two pieces

explanations.text: The minimum of two functions is realised by $\underline{\wedge}$, defined as $(x \to y) \wedge x = \min(x,y)$.
explanations.notes_quote: we can take: $F = F_{3,-1} \underline{\wedge} F_{-5,4}$ where $F_{3,-1}$ and $F_{-5,4}$ are constructed by the tree algorithm

explanations.text: The basic $\wedge$ in $\mathbb{F}^3$ computes $\max(0, x+y-1)$, not $\min(x,y)$. The defined $\underline{\wedge}$ is needed.
Each individual piece is constructed via Lemma 8.3 and the tree algorithm, then combined.
explanations.notes_quote: $F_{3,-1}$ and $F_{-5,4}$ are constructed by the tree algorithm

explanations.text: $\underline{\vee}$ realises $\max$, not $\min$. For the minimum of two pieces, $\underline{\wedge}$ is needed.

questions.text: For complex piecewise-linear functions, a wedge-shaped decomposition is used. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Each wedge is the $\min$ (using $\underline{\wedge}$) of two clamped linear functions
The overall function $f$ is the maximum (using $\underline{\vee}$) of all the wedges
A wedge-shaped function rises linearly to a peak then descends linearly back to zero
Every piecewise-linear function is automatically a single wedge

explanations.text: Each wedge is expressible as a minimum of two $f_{n,m}$ functions via $\underline{\wedge}$.
explanations.notes_quote: Each wedge can be expressed as a $\min$ (i.e., $\underline{\wedge}$) of two clamped linear functions

explanations.text: The full function is the maximum of all wedge pieces, realised by $\underline{\vee}$.
explanations.notes_quote: $f$ is the maximum (i.e., $\underline{\vee}$) of all the wedges

explanations.text: A wedge-shaped function rises to a peak then descends — hence the wedge terminology.
explanations.notes_quote: Each wedge is a function that: Rises linearly to a peak; Descends linearly back to zero

explanations.text: Complex piecewise-linear functions require multiple wedges; they are not all single wedges.

questions.source_ref: Section 6.2: De Morgan's Laws in F3
questions.text: Which of the following De Morgan's laws hold in $\mathbb{F}^3$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text:
$
eg(x \vee y) = 
eg x \wedge

eg(x \wedge y) =

eg(x \vee y) = 1 - \min(1, x+y) = \max(0, 1-(x+y)) = \max(0, 
eg x + 
eg y - 1) = (
eg x \wedge

explanations.text:
Verified: $
eg(x \wedge y) = 1 - \max(0, x+y-1) = \min(1, 1-(x+y-1)) = \min(1, 
eg x + 
eg y) = 
eg x \vee

eg(x \wedge y) = 1 - \max(0, x+y-1) = \min(1, (1-x)+(1-y)) = \min(1,

eg x \vee 
eg y)$

explanations.text:
This is incorrect; $
eg(x \vee y) = 
eg x \wedge

This is incorrect; $
eg(x \wedge y) = 
eg x \vee 
eg y$ (not $\wedge$). De Morgan swaps the connective.

questions.source_ref: Section 6.3: Implication as Disjunction in F3
questions.text: In $\mathbb{F}^3$, which of the following identities correctly relate implication to other connectives? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $(x \to y) = \neg x \vee y$
$(x \to y) = \min(1, \neg x + y)$
$(x \to y) = \max(0, y-x)$

explanations.text: In $\mathbb{F}^3$: $(x \to y) = \neg x \vee y = \min(1, (1-x)+y)$.
By the definition of $\mathbb{F}^3$ implication: $F_\to(x,y) = \min(1, 1-x+y)$.
Since $1-x = \neg x$ in $\mathbb{F}^3$: $\min(1, 1-x+y) = \min(1, \neg x + y)$.
$\max(0, y-x)$ does not equal $\min(1, 1-x+y)$ in general (e.g., at $x=0, y=0$: $\max(0,0)=0$ but $\min(1,1)=1$).

questions.text: A collection of intervals $P = \{I_1, \ldots, I_n\}$ is a partition of $[0,1]$ if which conditions hold? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The intervals are pairwise disjoint: $I_i \cap I_j = \varnothing$ for $i \neq j$
The intervals cover $[0,1]$: $\bigcup_{i=1}^n I_i = [0,1]$
Each interval must have positive length
All intervals must have the same length

explanations.text: Pairwise disjointness is the first condition for a partition.
The union covering $[0,1]$ is the second condition.
Single-point intervals (degenerate intervals) are allowed; positive length is not required.
The intervals can have any sizes; equal length is not a requirement.

questions.source_ref: Section 7.2: Lemma — Product of Two Partitions
questions.text: Let $P_1$ and $P_2$ be partitions of $[0,1]$. The intersection product $P = \{A \cap B : A \in P_1, B \in P_2\}$ is claimed to be a partition. Which parts of the proof are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Disjointness: if $A \cap B$ and $A' \cap B'$ overlap, then $A=A'$ and $B=B'$, so the two sets are equal
Coverage: any $x \in [0,1]$ belongs to some $A \in P_1$ and some $B \in P_2$, so $x \in A \cap B \in P$
This lemma is used in the proof of McNaughton's Theorem to refine partitions from subformulas
Coverage fails if $P_1$ and $P_2$ have different numbers of intervals

explanations.text:
If $(A \cap B) \cap (A' \cap B') 
eq \varnothing$, then $A \cap A' 
eq \varnothing$ forces $A=A'$ and $B \cap B' 
eq \varnothing$ forces $B=B'$.
explanations.notes_quote: Since $P_1$ is a partition, $A \cap A' \neq \varnothing$ forces $A = A'$; since $P_2$ is a partition, $B = B'$

explanations.text: Coverage of $P$: for any $x \in [0,1]$, find $A$ in $P_1$ and $B$ in $P_2$ containing $x$; then $x \in A \cap B$.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $x \in [0,1]$. Since $P_1$ covers $[0,1]$, there is some $A \in P_1$ with $x \in A$. Since $P_2$ covers $[0,1]$, there is some $B \in P_2$ with $x \in B$. Hence $x \in A \cap B \in P$.

explanations.text: In the proof of Lemma 8.2 for $G \wedge H$, the partitions from $G$ and $H$ are refined by their intersection product.
explanations.notes_quote: This lemma is used in the proof of McNaughton's Theorem — when combining the partition of $[0,1]$ from $\overline{G}$ and the partition from $\overline{H}$

explanations.text: Coverage holds regardless of the sizes of $P_1$ and $P_2$; both cover $[0,1]$ independently.

sections.title: Truth Functions — Plotting and Inflexion Points

questions.source_ref: Section 8.1: Key Formulas and Their Truth Functions
questions.text: Under $\mathbb{F}^3$, which of the following correctly give the truth functions for repeated conjunction and disjunction? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $p \wedge p$ has truth function $[2x-1]^1_0$
$p \vee p$ has truth function $[2x]^1_0$
The general pattern: $\underbrace{p \wedge \cdots \wedge p}_n$ has truth function $[nx-(n-1)]^1_0$
The general pattern: $\underbrace{p \vee \cdots \vee p}_n$ has truth function $[nx]^1_0$
$p \wedge p \wedge p \wedge p$ has truth function $[4x]^1_0$

explanations.text: The $n$-fold conjunction pattern: zero until $x = (n-1)/n$, then rises to 1.
The $n$-fold disjunction pattern: rises from 0 to 1 at $x = 1/n$, then stays at 1.
$p \wedge p \wedge p \wedge p$ has truth function $[4x-3]^1_0$ (not $[4x]^1_0$). $[4x]^1_0$ is the truth function of $p \vee p \vee p \vee p$.

questions.text: For the formula $F = (p \wedge p) \vee \neg p$ under $\mathbb{F}^3$, which of the following correctly describe the truth function? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For $x \leq 1/2$: $F(x) = 1 - x$ (descends from 1 to 1/2)
For $x > 1/2$: $F(x) = x$ (ascends from 1/2 to 1)
The function is V-shaped with minimum value $1/2$ at $x = 1/2$
The inflexion point is at $x = 1/3$

explanations.text: For $x \leq 1/2$: $p \wedge p = 0$, so $F = \min(1, 0 + (1-x)) = 1-x$.
The function is V-shaped: decreasing slope $-1$ until $x=1/2$, then increasing slope $+1$. Minimum is $1/2$.
explanations.notes_quote: Shape: V-shaped with minimum $\frac{1}{2}$ at $x = \frac{1}{2}$, values 1 at $x=0$ and $x=1$.

explanations.text: The inflexion point (gradient change) is at $x = 1/2$, not $x = 1/3$.

questions.text: For the formula $F = (p \vee p) \wedge p$ under $\mathbb{F}^3$, which of the following correctly describe its truth function? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F = 0$ for $x \leq 1/3$
$F = 3x - 1$ for $1/3 \leq x \leq 1/2$ (slope 3)
$F = x$ for $x > 1/2$ (slope 1)
There are two inflexion points: $x = 1/3$ and $x = 1/2$
$F = 2x - 1$ for $1/3 \leq x \leq 1/2$ (slope 2)

explanations.text: For $x \leq 1/2$: $p \vee p = 2x$, so $F = \max(0, 2x+x-1) = \max(0, 3x-1) = 0$ when $x \leq 1/3$.
For $1/3 < x \leq 1/2$: $F = 3x - 1$ (slope 3).
The slope changes at $x=1/3$ (from 0 to 3) and at $x=1/2$ (from 3 to 1).
The slope on $[1/3, 1/2]$ is 3, not 2. $F = 3x - 1$ there.

questions.text: Under $\mathbb{F}^3$, consider $F = p \vee p \vee p \vee p$ and $G = p \wedge p \wedge p \wedge p$. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F$ has truth function $[4x]^1_0$: slope 4 up to $x = 1/4$, then flat at 1
$G$ has truth function $[4x-3]^1_0$: flat 0 up to $x = 3/4$, then slope 4 up to 1
The inflexion point of $F$ is $x = 1/4$
The inflexion point of $G$ is $x = 1/4$
The inflexion point of $G$ is $x = 3/4$

explanations.text: $p \vee p \vee p \vee p = [4x]^1_0$: rises with slope 4 from 0, reaches 1 at $x=1/4$.
explanations.notes_quote: $(p \vee p \vee p \vee p)$: Truth function $[4x]^1_0$. Shape: Rises linearly with slope 4 from $x=0$ to $x = \frac{1}{4}$, then constant 1.

explanations.text: $p \wedge p \wedge p \wedge p = [4x-3]^1_0$: zero until $x=3/4$, then slope 4.
explanations.notes_quote: $(p \wedge p \wedge p \wedge p)$: Truth function $[4x-3]^1_0$. Shape: Zero from $x=0$ to $x = \frac{3}{4}$, then rises linearly with slope 4 to reach 1 at $x = 1$.

explanations.text: The slope of $F$ changes from 4 to 0 at $x = 1/4$.
The inflexion point of $G$ is $x = 3/4$, not $1/4$.
The slope of $G$ changes from 0 to 4 at $x = 3/4$.

questions.source_ref: Section 9 (SGT 8.6): Construct a Formula for a Piecewise Function
questions.text: Given the piecewise function in SGT 8.6 which is the maximum of $[2-3x]^1_0$, $[1-x]^1_0$, and $[3x-2]^1_0$, how is it represented as a formula? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F = G_1 \underline{\vee} G_2 \underline{\vee} G_3$ where $\underline{\vee}$ realises $\max$
$G_2 = \neg p$ since $[1-x]^1_0 = 1-x$ which is the truth function of $\neg p$
$G_3 = p \wedge p \wedge p$ since $[3x-2]^1_0$ is the truth function of $p \wedge p \wedge p$
$F = G_1 \underline{\wedge} G_2 \underline{\wedge} G_3$ where $\underline{\wedge}$ realises $\min$

explanations.text: Since $f = \max(G_1, G_2, G_3)$ and $\underline{\vee}$ realises $\max$, the formula is $F = G_1 \underline{\vee} G_2 \underline{\vee} G_3$.
$[1-x]^1_0 = 1-x$ on $[0,1]$, which is exactly the truth function of $\neg p$.
As established in SGT 8.4, $[3x-2]^1_0$ corresponds to $p \wedge p \wedge p$.
$\underline{\wedge}$ realises $\min$, not $\max$. Taking the minimum of the three pieces would give a different function.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly define key terms in Week 8? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\bot$ (falsum) is a formula with truth function constantly 0, e.g. $p \wedge \neg p$
$\top$ (verum) is a formula with truth function constantly 1, e.g. $p \vee \neg p$
An inflexion point is a value $x$ where the gradient (slope) of the truth function changes
Bézout's Identity states: for $\gcd(r,s)=1$, there exist $a,b \in \mathbb{Z}$ with $ar+bs=1$
A CPLIC function is one that is continuous, piecewise-linear, with integer coefficients

explanations.text: $\bot = p \wedge \neg p$ has truth function $\max(0, x+(1-x)-1) = \max(0,0) = 0$.
$\top = p \vee \neg p$ has truth function $\min(1, x+(1-x)) = \min(1,1) = 1$.
An inflexion point is where the slope changes in the piecewise linear truth function.
explanations.notes_quote: Inflexion point: A point $x$ in the domain of a truth function where the gradient (slope) changes

explanations.text: Bézout's Identity is used in the proof of Lemma 8.4 (sharp approximation).
explanations.notes_quote: Bézout's Identity: For integers $r, s$ with $\gcd(r,s)=1$, there exist $a, b \in \mathbb{Z}$ with $ar + bs = 1$

explanations.text: CPLIC is the abbreviation for the class of functions characterised by McNaughton's Theorem.
explanations.notes_quote: Continuous piecewise-linear function with integer coefficients (CPLIC): A continuous function $f : [0,1] \to [0,1]$ that is linear with integer slope and intercept on each sub-interval

questions.text: Regarding the defined operators $\underline{\wedge}$ and $\underline{\vee}$ in $\mathbb{F}^3$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $x \underline{\vee} y = (x \to y) \to y$
$\underline{\vee}$ realises $\max(x,y)$
$\underline{\wedge}$ realises $\min(x,y)$
$\underline{\wedge}$ and $\underline{\vee}$ are primitive connectives of $\mathbb{F}^3$, not defined operators

explanations.text: By definition: $x \underline{\vee} y = (x \to y) \to y$.
The truth function of $\underline{\vee}$ is $\max(x,y)$, the Gödel disjunction.
The truth function of $\underline{\wedge}$ is $\min(x,y)$, the Gödel conjunction.
$\underline{\wedge}$ and $\underline{\vee}$ are abbreviations (defined operators), not primitive connectives.
explanations.notes_quote: two additional binary operators $\underline{\wedge}$ and $\underline{\vee}$ as abbreviations (defined operators)

questions.text: McNaughton's Theorem (1 Variable) gives a complete characterisation of representable functions in $\mathbb{F}^3$. Which of the following correctly summarise the theorem and related results? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A function $f:[0,1]\to[0,1]$ is representable in $\mathbb{F}^3$ iff it is a CPLIC function
Lemma 8.2 proves the 'only if' direction (representable $\Rightarrow$ CPLIC) by structural induction
Lemma 8.3 and the Tree Algorithm prove the 'if' direction (every $[nx+m]^1_0$ is representable)
McNaughton's Theorem was proved in 1963 by Saul Kripke
The theorem implies that $\mathbb{F}^3$ can represent any continuous real-valued function on $[0,1]$

explanations.text: This is the precise statement of McNaughton's Theorem (1 variable).
explanations.notes_quote: McNaughton's Theorem (1 variable): A function $f : [0,1] \to [0,1]$ is the truth function of a propositional formula in $\mathbb{F}^3$ iff $f$ is a CPLIC function

explanations.text: Lemma 8.2 establishes the 'only if' direction by structural induction on formula size.
explanations.notes_quote: Lemma 8.2: Every truth function of a one-variable $\mathbb{F}^3$ formula is a CPLIC function (proved by induction)

explanations.text: Lemma 8.3 shows every $[nx+m]^1_0$ is representable; combining pieces gives the full 'if' direction.
explanations.notes_quote: Lemma 8.3: For any $n, m \in \mathbb{Z}$, $f_{n,m}(x) = [nx+m]^1_0$ is the truth function of some formula $F_{n,m}$

explanations.text: McNaughton's Theorem was proved by Robert McNaughton in 1951, not by Saul Kripke (who introduced Kripke semantics for modal logic in 1963).
Only CPLIC functions (piecewise linear with integer coefficients) are representable — not all continuous functions. For example, $f(x) = x^2$ is continuous but not representable.
